CSCE 438 Syllabus

Distributed Systems

Instructor Details

Tue/Th: 11:30am-12:30pm

Catalog Description

Distributed Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. Principles and techniques for engineering

distributed systems with topics including communication, concurrency,

programming paradigms, naming, managing shared state, caching, synchronization,

reaching agreement, fault tolerance, security, middleware and distributed

applications; design, implement and debug large software systems. Prerequisite:

CSCE 313; junior or senior classification, or approval of instructor.

Additional Course Details

The objectives for this course are for students to gain an understanding of the

principles and practices (e.g., locking, concurrency, scheduling, communication

across networks, fault tolerance, protocols, remote procedure calls, file sharing,

reliable system design, load balancing, protection and security) behind the design of

distributed processing systems, such as Google, Facebook, Amazon, Yahoo and

Microsoft, and to gain practical experience designing, implementing, and debugging

distributed systems. Honors students will evaluate newly proposed distributed

systems technologies, e.g., blockchain, distributed ledger.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 313; junior or senior classification, or approval

of instructor.

Restrictions: None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Implement and structure distributed systems programs

systems technologies, e.g., blockchain, distributed ledger.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 313; junior or senior classification, or approval

of instructor.

Restrictions: None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Implement and structure distributed systems programs

Write computer programs that interoperate using well-defined protocols

Employ and create common paradigms (remote procedure calls and

message oriented middleware) for easing the task of distributed systems

programming

Debug highly concurrent code that spans multiple programs running on

multiple machines

Reason about distributed algorithms, locking, synchronization and

concurrency, scheduling and replication

Evaluate newly proposed distributed systems technologies (e.g., blockchain,

distributed ledgers) from the perspective of suitability to a particular

application.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Distributed Systems

Authors:  Maarten van Steen, Andrew S. Tanenbaum

Publication Date:  January 2025

Edition:  4.0.3

URL for Resource:  https://www.distributed-systems.net/index.php/books/ds4/

This material Is: Required

Research papers distributed in class

Grading Policy

Homework: 10%

Machine Problems (MPs): 40%

Midterm Exam 1: 15%

Midterm Exam 2: 15%

Final Exam: 15%

Report: 5%

The grading scale will be: A ≥ 90% > B ≥ 80% > C ≥ 70% > D ≥ 60% > F.

Assignments: there will be 5 Machine Problems (MPs) involving C/C++ and Java

programming. There will be 6 homework assignments. The exams will be in class, closed

book, closed notes, pencil-paper based, with a "cheat-sheet" provided by the instructor.

The Final Exam is cumulative, with 80% of the questions covering material from the

Midterm 2.

Report: Students will write a report on a new distributed systems technology, e.g.,

blockchain, distributed ledger.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

One Machine Problem (MP) submission will be accepted with no penalty for up to 4 days late.

blockchain, distributed ledger.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

One Machine Problem (MP) submission will be accepted with no penalty for up to 4 days late.

The allowable 4 late days cannot be split among multiple Machine Problems. Allowable late

days are for emergencies. All other late Machine Problems will be accepted with a penalty of

20% per late day.

No late submissions will be accepted for homework and Report.

AI Statement

According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct,

plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words

without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use

of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) text generators and natural language processing tools

(colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video, and image

generators should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission

of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to, i)

Creating or revising drafts; ii) Editing your work; iii) Reviewing a peer's work. This

excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers,

which are acceptable.
